description: Engine-agnostic privilege translation: the vendor-neutral domain grants contract (Unity Catalog vocabulary) -> Snowflake privileges. Read by BOTH the Snowflake Terraform (jsondecode + lookup) and the Python cross-backend consistency test (scripts/snowflake_backend.py), so the two enforcement backends provably derive from one contract. See docs/adr/0011-snowflake-enforcement-backend.md.
notes: The UC privilege set (scripts/validate_domains.py) is the canonical neutral vocabulary because it is the existing contract and the richer of the two; the UC backend maps it identity, Snowflake maps it here.

Every mapping is capability-preserving: an abstract privilege that confers read/write/admin maps to Snowflake privilege(s) that confer the same effective access. This invariant is asserted by tests/test_snowflake_backend.py (independently, per engine) so a mistranslation is caught.

A fragment is {privileges[], scope, access}. 'scope' tells the Terraform module WHERE to attach the Snowflake privilege, since Snowflake data-access privileges live on tables, not on the schema/database container. Scopes: 'self' = on the object itself; 'future_and_existing_tables' = ON ALL + FUTURE TABLES IN the object (both, to cover existing and new tables). 'access' documents the conferred capability (read|write|admin|usage; usage = traversal only).

UC privileges with no faithful Snowflake stage equivalent (external-location BROWSE / CREATE_FOREIGN_SECURABLE / CREATE_MANAGED_STORAGE; catalog/schema-level volume privileges) are intentionally omitted: the coverage test fails loudly if a domain ever grants one, forcing a deliberate mapping decision rather than a silent over/under-grant.
